preamble: The shift from a voiceless velar stop [k] to a voiceless palatal stop [c] represents the initial stage of palatalization before front vowels or glides. This 'k > c' stage is often a transitory step toward further affrication (as in Slavic or Romance) but can be phonemically stabilized as a distinct palatal series, as seen in Hungarian.

examples.note: Hungarian maintains a stable phonemic palatal stop /c/ (written 'ty') that historically developed via palatalization.
Intermediate stage before the shift to [tʃ] or [ts] in different Romance branches.
The First Slavic Palatalization involved an intermediate [c] stage before [tʃ].

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
